---
title: "6 · Memory & disturbance"
---

The soil state carries **memory**: what it does next depends not only on today's forcing but on the
path it took to get here. Two forms of that memory are physically real, are what the seismic signal is
most sensitive to, and are **absent from the state as currently integrated**. This chapter sets out the
physics, why the present model cannot represent it, and how the twin incorporates it — with the
observation that the same measurement, **dv/v, sees all of it**, which turns the problem into one of
*attribution*.

## Where the state is memoryless

The [hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd) integrates two reservoirs — root-zone storage and
water-table head — so the state *does* carry cumulative-forcing memory. But two path dependencies are
missing:

- **The retention curve is single-valued.** One $\theta_{wp}/\theta_{fc}/\theta_{sat}$ envelope governs
  both drying and wetting, so a given storage always maps to the same suction and the same stiffness. In
  real soil it does not: the suction needed to *drain* a pore is larger than the suction at which it
  *fills*, so water content plotted against matric potential traces a **loop, not a line**.
- **The moisture-to-velocity map is instantaneous and elastic.** The petrophysics of
  [hydromechanics](03b-physics-hydromechanics.qmd) converts the *current* moisture and water table to
  $V_s$ and dv/v with no rate term and no damage term. That chapter already writes the missing physics —
  the dynamic-capillarity term $\tau(S_w)\,\partial_t S_w$ whose coefficient differs between wetting and
  drying — and notes that the coded $\delta v/v = S_\theta\,\Delta\theta$ is only its local
  linearization.

There is also **no disturbance state at all**. Earthquakes, liquefaction, landslides, and wildfire
appear in the twin only as *downstream hazard consumers* of the water table and $V_s$ — never as inputs
that change the soil itself.

## Capillary hysteresis: drying is not wetting

Hysteresis in the water-retention curve is a principal source of **soil-moisture memory**: the same
moisture content sits at a different suction, and therefore a different effective stress and stiffness,
depending on whether the soil is on a drying or a wetting path. The implementable representation is the
**Kool–Parker scanning-curve model** [@koolparker1987], which scales the secondary and higher-order
scanning curves off the main drying and wetting limbs and carries a single extra state — the **last
reversal point** — so the retention relation "remembers" where the last wetting or drying began.

![Capillary hysteresis, implemented (`src/models/hysteresis.py`). **Left:** a seasonal moisture path
traces a *loop* between the main drying and wetting curves, not a single line; a mid-summer storm opens
an inner **scanning loop** — the soil's memory of that reversal. **Right:** the same path in the twin's
observable, $V_s(\theta)$, through the identical Hertz–Mindlin map used elsewhere: at equal moisture the
**drying limb is stiffer** (higher velocity, higher dv/v) than the wetting limb, and both depart from
the single-valued curve the current model assumes.](assets/hysteresis_loop.png){#fig-hysteresis}

::: {.callout-note}
## Agroseismology: reading the hysteretic state seismically
[@shi2026agroseismology] (a Denolle-lab *Science* study) applies distributed acoustic sensing to an
experimental farm and inverts seismic velocity for soil saturation through a **physics-based
hydromechanical model** — the same effective-stress / Hertz–Mindlin family the twin uses. It also shows
the second theme of this chapter directly: **tillage and compaction are a disturbance** that disrupts
the capillary pore network, slows drainage, and increases evaporation — a change in the hydromechanical
response that the velocity field registers. Hysteresis and disturbance are the two faces of soil memory,
and dv/v is sensitive to both.
:::

## Landscape disturbance: a step change, then a slow recovery

Disturbances perturb the soil's hydraulic and mechanical properties abruptly and then **recover over
weeks to years** — a memory the state must carry as an evolving variable, not a permanent edit.

| disturbance | what it changes | recovery timescale |
|---|---|---|
| **earthquake / liquefaction** | near-surface velocity drops; pore structure and pressure disturbed | **log-linear, days → years** [@illien2025recovery; @wang2021healing] |
| **wildfire** | hydrophobic layer → infiltration and $K_s$ fall, runoff rises | ~2–4 (up to ~6) years |
| **agricultural (tillage)** | capillary connectivity disrupted → slower drainage | season → years [@shi2026agroseismology] |
| **landslide / ground failure** | remolding, cohesion loss | months → years |

The coseismic case is the best characterised. After shaking, the near-surface velocity drops and then
**heals log-linearly in time** — "slow dynamics" — and the recovery *rate* appears to be an intrinsic
property of the substrate rather than of the shaking intensity [@illien2025recovery; @wang2021healing].
Crucially for a twin that also tracks water, [@illien2022gorkha] showed that the post-Gorkha dv/v signal
is a **superposition of transient damage and groundwater drainage**, and separated the two — which is
exactly the problem the twin faces in reverse.

The twin represents each disturbance as an **event that sets a memory state** — a velocity deficit $D$
for coseismic damage, a property perturbation $\Phi$ (hydrophobicity, $K_s$ multiplier, cohesion) for
surface disturbances — which then **relaxes on that process's own timescale**. The forward velocity
becomes $\delta v/v = \text{elastic}(\theta, h) + D$, and the water budget's infiltration and retention
terms carry the $\Phi$ perturbation.

## Multi-scale: a timescale per process, a footprint per event

The processes span orders of magnitude in *both* space and time, and the design turns that into an
advantage rather than a difficulty.

- **Time.** Each memory is a state variable with its **own relaxation constant** — storm-scale for
  hysteresis, log-linear over years for coseismic healing, multi-year for fire — advanced by
  operator-splitting: the fast hysteresis inside the water-budget step, the slow damage and disturbance
  as an outer relaxation.
- **Space.** Disturbances are **patchy and defined at their native scale**: shaking from USGS ShakeMap
  (~km), burn severity from MTBS (30 m), landslide inventory polygons, liquefaction susceptibility
  derived from the $V_s30$ layer the twin already carries. Each enters as an **event field** reprojected
  through the forward-operator [data plane](02b-cyberinfrastructure.qmd) — native resolution, no
  regridding, sub-grid susceptibility fractions.

## Attribution is the goal

Because dv/v responds to hydrology, coseismic damage, *and* surface disturbance at once, a measured
change is ambiguous — and the twin's job is to **partition it**. This is where the memory states earn
their place in the [assimilation](04-assimilation.qmd): the control vector is **augmented** with the
damage state $D$ and the disturbance perturbation $\Phi$, the coda kernels
([evaluation](05-state-evaluation.qmd)) supply the **spatial** footprint that distinguishes where a
change originates, and the **distinct relaxation timescales** supply the temporal discriminant that
separates a seasonal-elastic hydrology signal from a log-healing damage signal from a multi-year fire
signal. The BLUE innovation then divides a dv/v anomaly among the three. It is the generalisation of the
damage-versus-drainage decomposition [@illien2022gorkha] to a full state estimator, and it is the
reason the twin instruments the subsurface with seismic velocity in the first place.

## Status

**Implemented.** Capillary hysteresis (`src/models/hysteresis.py`, `tests/test_hysteresis.py`) is
integrated and on by default in `forecast_soil_state`. The loop in @fig-hysteresis is its output,
coupled to the twin's own Hertz–Mindlin velocity map, and the memory is carried through the
**integrated** state — the forecast advances the per-cell reversal point along the modelled moisture
trajectory, so the vadose dv/v is path-dependent (the same θ reached by drying versus wetting gives a
different velocity).

**Planned (milestone: v0.6 — Memory & disturbance).** The remaining pieces are identified hooks, not
yet built, in this order: the petrophysical map's coseismic damage-and-healing term; the
surface-disturbance property classes (wildfire, agricultural, landslide); a disturbance event catalog
on the data plane; and the augmented-attribution assimilation that partitions a dv/v anomaly among
hydrology, damage, and disturbance.
